title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a wildlife officer called Rachel talking about a neighbourhood hedgehog project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a wildlife officer called Rachel talking about a neighbourhood hedgehog project. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A wildlife officer called Rachel talks about a neighbourhood hedgehog project.
extracts.audio_script: Hi everyone. My name’s Rachel and I coordinate Hedgehog Haven, a project finding out how hedgehogs move through neighbourhood gardens. Our project area may seem like an ordinary group of streets, but much of it was built on old [9]railway land[/9]. Long strips of bushes remained after the tracks disappeared, and we think these provide useful routes for wildlife. Residents told us they occasionally saw hedgehogs at night, but we needed more reliable information before recommending improvements.||The first stage of the project was to discover which animal local people wanted help to protect. There were suggestions about bats and garden birds, but the most frequent reports were of [10]hedgehogs[/10], whose numbers people felt had declined. Volunteers do not need scientific experience. At their first session, they learn how to look for [11]footprints[/11] rather than trying to catch or touch animals. Finding signs of movement causes far less disturbance and can provide accurate information over several nights.||The equipment is simple. Each participating garden receives a small [12]tracking tunnel[/12], a covered box containing harmless ink and paper, which records marks when an animal walks through. It is checked in the morning, so there is no need for volunteers to stay awake at night. Some people hope to attract animals with a bowl of milk, but this is not suitable. Where food is used, the recommended choice is ordinary [13]cat food[/13] in small amounts, alongside fresh water. We provide clear instructions and ask residents not to change their normal garden routine too much during the survey.||Collecting information is only useful if it results in action. Hedgehogs travel between spaces to find food and shelter, but solid barriers can stop them. The simplest improvement many homes can make is creating small openings in [14]garden fences[/14], with the owner's permission. Volunteers record possible routes on a shared [15]wildlife map[/15], which will show where animals are found and where connections might be improved. Names and exact addresses will not be made public. The map will be used by our conservation officers when advising residents and the council.||We also offer activities for people who want to learn more. Last year families attended an afternoon craft event, but this spring the quickest event to fill has been our guided [16]evening walk[/16], when an expert will explain how nocturnal animals behave. Volunteers completing the full survey period receive a [17]conservation certificate[/17], acknowledging their contribution to local research. Finally, injured animals found by residents are currently taken a long distance for care. Money from our summer garden day will help create a small [18]hedgehog hospital[/18] in partnership with a local vet. Please take a survey leaflet and sign-up card before you leave.

dialogue.speaker: rachel
dialogue.text: Hi everyone. My name’s Rachel and I coordinate Hedgehog Haven, a project finding out how hedgehogs move through neighbourhood gardens. Our project area may seem like an ordinary group of streets, but much of it was built on old railway land. Long strips of bushes remained after the tracks disappeared, and we think these provide useful routes for wildlife. Residents told us they occasionally saw hedgehogs at night, but we needed more reliable information before recommending improvements.||The first stage of the project was to discover which animal local people wanted help to protect. There were suggestions about bats and garden birds, but the most frequent reports were of hedgehogs, whose numbers people felt had declined. Volunteers do not need scientific experience. At their first session, they learn how to look for footprints rather than trying to catch or touch animals. Finding signs of movement causes far less disturbance and can provide accurate information over several nights.||The equipment is simple. Each participating garden receives a small tracking tunnel, a covered box containing harmless ink and paper, which records marks when an animal walks through. It is checked in the morning, so there is no need for volunteers to stay awake at night. Some people hope to attract animals with a bowl of milk, but this is not suitable. Where food is used, the recommended choice is ordinary cat food in small amounts, alongside fresh water. We provide clear instructions and ask residents not to change their normal garden routine too much during the survey.||Collecting information is only useful if it results in action. Hedgehogs travel between spaces to find food and shelter, but solid barriers can stop them. The simplest improvement many homes can make is creating small openings in garden fences, with the owner's permission. Volunteers record possible routes on a shared wildlife map, which will show where animals are found and where connections might be improved. Names and exact addresses will not be made public. The map will be used by our conservation officers when advising residents and the council.||We also offer activities for people who want to learn more. Last year families attended an afternoon craft event, but this spring the quickest event to fill has been our guided evening walk, when an expert will explain how nocturnal animals behave. Volunteers completing the full survey period receive a conservation certificate, acknowledging their contribution to local research. Finally, injured animals found by residents are currently taken a long distance for care. Money from our summer garden day will help create a small hedgehog hospital in partnership with a local vet. Please take a survey leaflet and sign-up card before you leave.
